content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 8, 1979

Mr. Robert Martinez
Deputy District Attorney
County of Los Angeles
18000 Criminal Courts Bldg.
210 W. Temple Street
Los Angeles, CA 90012

Dear Mr. Martinez:

This letter is in response to your request for an
analysis of sanctions available to civil and criminal
prosecutors in connection with violations of the disqualifi-
cation provision of the Political Reform Act and conflict
of interest codes adopted pursuant thereto.

The Act provides in Section 87100-1/ that no public
official shall make, participate in the making or use his
official position to influence the making of governmental
decisions in which he has a financial interest. Section 87102
provides that injunctive relief is the exclusive remedy for
violation of this section of the Act. However, the remedies
against those public officials who violate a conflict of
interest code are far more expansive.

The general prohibition contained in Section 87100 is
applicable to all state and local employees. In addition,
Section 87300 requires every state and local agency to adopt
a conflict of interest code containing a similar disqualifi-
cation prohibition to be applied to those filing officers specifi-
cally included in the code. Section 87300 provides that if a
violation of the code occurs, it shall be considered a violation
of the Political Reform Act. Thus, the civil, criminal and
administrative remedies provided for by the Act, and briefly

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise noted.

Mr. Robert Martinez                                                Page Two
August 8, 1979


described below, are available for violations of the dis-
qualification provision of a conflict of interest code.

     Section 91000(a) specifically provides that "Any person
who knowingly or willfully violates any provision of this
title (Act) is guilty of a misdemeanor."  (parentheses added.)
With respect to civil remedies, Government Code Section 91005(b)
states:

     Any designated employee who realizes an economic
     benefit as a result of a violation of a disqualifi-
     cation provision of a Conflict of Interest Code is
     liable in a civil action brought by the civil prose-
     cutor or by a person residing within the jurisdiction
     for an amount up to three times the value of the
     benefit.

     In addition to civil and criminal remedies, Section 83116
and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18361 permits civil prosecutors to
bring actions before the Commission for a hearing and the
issuance of administrative sanctions including fines of up
to $2,000 for each conflict of interest violation.

     Lastly, Section 91003.5 permits an agency to discipline
or dismiss its employees consistent with any applicable civil
service or other personnel laws, regulations and procedures.

     I hope that this analysis is helpful. If I may be of
further assistance, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Dwight F. Bickerson
                                        Legal Counsel
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

DFD:nrw

State of California

Memorandum

To : Advice Files                                    Date : Feb. 14, 1979


                                                     M-79-082

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Sarah Cameron

Subject: Automatic Overdraft Protection/Balance-Plus Checking Accounts


     On January 30, 1979 I talked with Lana Newton of the
United California Bank and asked her about their "Balance Plus"
checking accounts which allow the person holding the account to
write checks for up to $500 more than the amount in the account.
She said that the standards they apply to applicants for these
accounts are the same as those for applicants for a Master Charge
or a Visa card.  In fact, she said, they use the same form for
applications for balance plus accounts, bank charge cards and
small personal loans.  She said that the interest charges are the
same on balance plus accounts as they are for bank charge cards,
i.e., 18% annually, but that they are slightly less for un-
secured personal loans under $3000 -- 17%.

     Ted had talked with the Legal Division and had already
tentatively concluded that balance plus accounts should be
treated the same way as bank credit cards and should not be
deemed to create "loans" under the Act.  After this discussion
with Ms. Newton, I agreed.
